title: SEGMENT 13 — What they're taking: the immunosuppression cocktail

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — What they're taking: the immunosuppression cocktail
Let's talk about drugs.
Because the patient isn't just walking around with an edited pig kidney and an unmodified immune system.
The patient is on a pharmacological regimen that is, by transplant standards, unusual and aggressive.
Understanding the regimen tells you something important about which immune problems are still live, even after all the genetic engineering.
Standard human-to-human kidney transplant is, in most centers, managed with a three-drug cocktail.
The cornerstone is tacrolimus, a calcineurin inhibitor — a drug that blocks T-cell activation.
The second is mycophenolate, an antiproliferative drug that blocks B and T cell division.
The third is a low-dose corticosteroid like prednisone, which suppresses inflammation broadly.
That triple combination has been the standard since the 1990s, and it works well enough for human-to-human transplant.
For xenotransplant, that standard triple is not enough.
The pig organ, even with the triple knockout and human transgenes, still presents some antigens the recipient's T cells can recognize.
Without additional suppression, you would get T-cell-mediated rejection within weeks.
The xeno protocol that has been used in the 2024 kidney cases adds two more drugs to the conventional cocktail.
The first is belatacept, a fusion protein that blocks something called CD28 — a key costimulatory receptor that T cells need to fully activate.
Belatacept is already approved for human-to-human kidney transplant in patients who can't tolerate tacrolimus; in xeno, it's used in addition to tacrolimus, layered on top.
The second, and more interesting, addition is an experimental class of antibody called anti-CD40L — sometimes shorthanded as anti-CD154 or as a monoclonal called tegoprubart or similar agents.
CD40L is a molecule on T cells that, when it binds CD40 on antigen-presenting cells, drives a major arm of the immune response — the so-called costimulatory pathway.
Blocking CD40L is a way of cutting the second signal that T cells need to commit to attack.
The drug class has been studied for human autoimmune disease for decades but ran into safety problems — earlier generations caused thrombosis.
Newer-generation anti-CD40L antibodies have been engineered to avoid that problem and are now in human trials specifically for xenotransplant.
So the regimen is, roughly: tacrolimus, plus belatacept, plus anti-CD40L, plus low-dose steroids.
Four drugs.
Each one targeting a different part of the T-cell activation pathway.
The reasoning is: layered, redundant blockade of T-cell function, on top of an already heavily-engineered organ.
The hope is that this combination — once the antibody and complement problems are out of the way thanks to the gene editing — is enough to keep the cellular immune response in check.
It is, as you can imagine, a heavy regimen.
Each of these drugs has side effects.
Patients are at significant risk of opportunistic infection.
They have to be carefully monitored.
But this is the price of admission, currently, for a xenotransplant.
Over time, as the field gains experience, the regimen will be refined.
Some drugs may be dropped.
Doses may be lowered.
The goal, eventually, is a regimen no more intense than standard human-to-human transplant.
We are not there yet.
